title: SEGMENT 8 — Scaffold materials: collagen, polymers, alginate, and ECM

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Scaffold materials: collagen, polymers, alginate, and ECM
Let me back up and talk in more detail about the scaffold part of the recipe, because we've been throwing around the word without saying much about what scaffolds are actually made of.
The choice of scaffold material is, in many ways, the central engineering decision in any tissue engineering project.
Different materials have different properties.
Different applications need different properties.
There is no universal scaffold.
Let me run through the main categories.
Natural polymers — collagen and friends.
Collagen is the most abundant protein in your body.
It's the structural protein that makes up most of the extracellular matrix in skin, bone, tendon, cartilage, and almost every other tissue.
As a scaffold material, collagen has huge advantages.
It is biocompatible — your cells recognize it, attach to it, and behave normally on it.
It is biodegradable — your body's enzymes will slowly break it down over weeks to months, allowing the engineered tissue to remodel and replace the scaffold with native matrix.
It can be formed into sponges, sheets, gels, fibers — pretty much any geometry you want.
It is sourced typically from bovine or porcine tissue and purified, though there are now recombinant human collagens available.
The main limitations are mechanical — collagen scaffolds tend to be soft and weak unless cross-linked — and source-related — animal-derived collagen has some immunogenicity concerns.

Other natural polymers in use include alginate, derived from brown algae, which forms a gel when cross-linked with calcium ions and is widely used in bioprinting bio-inks; gelatin, which is denatured collagen, often modified to make it photocrosslinkable (more on that later); hyaluronic acid, a glycosaminoglycan abundant in cartilage and connective tissue, useful for soft tissue applications; fibrin, derived from blood clotting proteins, often used as a tissue sealant and scaffold; and chitosan,
derived from crustacean shells, used in wound healing and some cartilage applications.
Synthetic polymers — PLGA and friends.
The other major category is synthetic biodegradable polymers, of which the most important is poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid), or PLGA.

PLGA is a copolymer of lactic acid and glycolic acid, both of which are natural metabolites — the same lactic acid your muscles produce during exercise — and so when PLGA degrades, it breaks down into innocuous byproducts.
PLGA has been used in dissolving sutures for decades, which means its biocompatibility and degradation profile are very well understood.
By adjusting the ratio of lactic to glycolic acid, you can tune the degradation rate from weeks to years.

By adjusting molecular weight and processing conditions, you can make PLGA into films, fibers, sponges, microspheres, or 3D-printable structures with whatever mechanical properties your application needs.

Other synthetic polymers include polycaprolactone (PCL), which degrades more slowly and is used in applications like bone scaffolds; polyethylene glycol (PEG), which is hydrophilic and used in hydrogel formulations; and polyglycolic acid (PGA), the precursor of PLGA.
Atala's original bladder scaffold was a PGA-based mesh coated with collagen.
The trade-off between natural and synthetic polymers is roughly this.
Natural polymers are biologically friendly — cells like them, they degrade by biological mechanisms — but they're harder to control mechanically and are subject to batch-to-batch variability.

Synthetic polymers are reproducible, tunable, and mechanically robust, but they're less biologically active — cells don't recognize them as natural matrix, so you often have to coat synthetic scaffolds with collagen or other biological molecules to get cells to attach properly.
Most modern scaffolds are some kind of hybrid — synthetic backbone for mechanical control, natural coating for biological compatibility.
Decellularized ECM.
We covered this in the previous segment, but it bears repeating that decellularized native extracellular matrix is itself a scaffold material — arguably the most biologically authentic one.
Decellularized small intestinal submucosa, marketed as products like SIS or Surgisis, is used clinically as a wound healing patch.
Decellularized dermis is used in burn surgery.
Decellularized heart valves are used in valve replacement.
The ECM scaffolds carry over the native architecture and the bound growth factors of the source tissue, which can powerfully direct cell behavior.
The downsides are source variability and the limited supply of human tissue.
Hydrogels.
Hydrogels are scaffolds that are mostly water, with a small fraction of crosslinked polymer providing structure.
They're soft, like jello, and they can be tuned to have mechanical properties similar to soft tissues — brain, fat, muscle.
Most bio-inks for 3D bioprinting are hydrogels.

The most important ones in the field today include GelMA — gelatin methacrylate, a modified gelatin that can be cured into a stable gel by exposure to UV light in the presence of a photoinitiator — and various alginate-based hydrogels which cross-link upon exposure to calcium.
We'll get into bio-inks in detail in a few segments.
What matters for our purposes is that the scaffold is not a passive frame.
The scaffold material affects cell adhesion, cell signaling, mechanical behavior, degradation rate, and the eventual tissue's properties.
Choosing a scaffold material is choosing a chemistry and a mechanics for the developing tissue.
Get it wrong and the cells refuse to cooperate.
Get it right and the cells do their job.
